name: dumbbell step-up lunge
instructions: Stand in front of a step or platform with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing your sides.
Place your right foot on the step, ensuring your entire foot is on the surface.
Push through your right heel and lift your body up onto the step, bringing your left foot up as well.
Once both feet are on the step, lower your left foot back down to the starting position, keeping your right foot on the step.
Repeat the movement, alternating which foot you step up with each time.
Continue for the desired number of repetitions.

Stand in front of a step or platform with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing your sides. Place your right foot on the step, ensuring your entire foot is on the surface. Push through your right heel and lift your body up onto the step, bringing your left foot up as well. Once both feet are on the step, lower your left foot back down to the starting position, keeping your right foot on the step. Repeat the movement, alternating which foot you step up with each time. Continue for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
